events.note: Dump tool execution — comsvcs.dll MiniDump via rundll32 (LOLBin technique)
Sysmon 10: ProcessAccess on lsass — GrantedAccess mask determines dumper capability
Dump file created on disk — Sysmon 11 FileCreate catches the output

mutation_hints.objective: Extract NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets from LSASS process memory
detection_anchors: Field match: GrantedAccess=0x1fffff (PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS) → use 0x1010 (PROCESS_VM_READ|PROCESS_QUERY_LIMITED_INFORMATION) — the minimum rights needed for memory read; many rules only alert on 0x1fffff or 0x143a
Field match: SourceImage=rundll32.exe → change to C:\Windows\Temp\svchost.exe (staged tool renamed to appear legitimate) or C:\ProgramData\Intel\sysinfo.exe
Field match: CommandLine contains comsvcs.dll → use a different dump method: NanoDump injects via reflective DLL (no comsvcs), or use SilentProcessExit dump via WerFault.exe
Field match: CallTrace contains comsvcs.dll or UNKNOWN → different injection / dump technique has a different call stack; removing UNKNOWN removes a common alert trigger
Field: TargetFilename in C:\Windows\Temp\ → move dump to C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\WER\ or C:\Users\Public\Music\ — less commonly monitored paths
Field match: Image=rundll32.exe → use C:\Windows\System32\rdrleakdiag.exe (native LSASS dump tool, signed) or procdump64.exe renamed
Field: ParentImage=cmd.exe → change to powershell.exe or explorer.exe — direct spawns from cmd are more suspicious
